title: p-adic Hyperbolicity of Shimura Varieties
speaker: Abishek Oswal
abstract: A classical theorem due to Borel states that every holomorphic map from a poly-punctured disk into a Shimura variety (with torsion-free level structure) extends holomorphically across the punctures to the minimal compactification. As a consequence, every holomorphic map from a complex algebraic variety into such a variety is algebraic. In this talk, I shall discuss a p-adic version of the above result. For Shimura varieties of abelian type, this is joint work with Shankar, Zhu and Patel and for exceptional Shimura varieties this is based on ongoing work with Bakker, Shankar and Yao.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Complex Borel hyperbolicity template
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Baily-Borel algebraicity and compactification of arithmetic quotients of Hermitian symmetric domains, then states Borel’s extension theorem from polypunctured disks and its consequence that holomorphic maps from algebraic varieties to such quotients are algebraic.

proof_stages.label: p-adic analogues and main theorem statements
proof_stages.summary: Using canonical models of Shimura varieties over their reflex fields, the speaker formulates the p-adic extension/algebraization question and states affirmative results for abelian-type Shimura varieties, large-prime compact exceptional Shimura varieties, and compact geometric period images.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Ag and all-good/all-bad dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: For abelian-type Shimura varieties the proof is reduced to the moduli space Ag with level structure, and an analytic map from the punctured disk is shown, via annuli and l-adic monodromy, to land entirely in either the good-reduction locus or its complement.

proof_stages.label: Good-reduction image lies over one isogeny class
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the map lands in the good-reduction locus, formal models of annuli with tree-like special fibers force the pulled-back l-adic local system to be isotrivial mod p, so each annular image lies in the tube over a single mod-p isogeny class.

proof_stages.label: Rapoport-Zink lift and de Rham trivialization
proof_stages.summary: The tube over the fixed isogeny class is uniformized by a Rapoport-Zink space, and simple connectedness of the Berkovich punctured disk lifts the map there, where the relevant de Rham cohomology bundle with connection becomes trivial.

proof_stages.label: p-adic Hodge extension and filling the puncture
proof_stages.summary: The trivialized de Rham data and the p-adic Riemann-Hilbert correspondence extend the pulled-back crystalline local system and Hodge filtration across the puncture, allowing the induced period/flag map, and hence the original map, to be filled in.

proof_stages.label: Bad-reduction boundary induction for the abelian case
proof_stages.summary: For maps lying in the bad-reduction locus, the same dichotomy places the image in the tube over a single Borel boundary stratum, after which toroidal compactification and a retraction to a lower-dimensional good-reduction problem give an inductive treatment.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional and period-image replacement strategy
proof_stages.summary: In the absence of modular interpretations and global Rapoport-Zink uniformization for exceptional Shimura varieties, the proof replaces those ingredients by large-prime integral canonical models and recent p-adic Hodge/prismatic results extending crystalline local systems and controlling the resulting F-crystals.

examples.name: Upper half-plane modular curves
examples.note: The quotient of the upper half-plane by congruence subgroups is recalled as the modular curve parametrizing elliptic curves with level structure, making the general Hermitian-symmetric quotient picture concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker presents the upper half-plane quotients as standard examples of modular curves and states their moduli interpretation, without carrying out a computation or verification on them

passes.reason: The upper-half-plane quotients are standard locally symmetric modular-curve examples used illustratively, not to refute a claim.

quotients of the upper half-plane are presented as standard arithmetic quotients of the maximally symmetric Hermitian symmetric domain H
The upper half-plane is a Hermitian symmetric domain uniformizing the standard modular-curve family, used only as a guiding example.

used as standard illustrative modular/Shimura quotients of the symmetric upper half-plane, not to refute a claim

examples.name: Level-one modular curve and an essential singularity
examples.note: Identifying the level-one modular curve with the affine line and using maps such as z ↦ exp(1/z) from the punctured disk shows why the torsion-free hypothesis is necessary for Borel-type extension statements.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the level-one curve concretely as C with compactification P1 and exhibits z↦exp(1/z) on the punctured disk to show non-extension via an essential singularity

passes.reason: The minimal level-one curve Y(1)≅A¹ with an essential-singularity map such as exp(1/z) shows Borel extension can fail without torsion-free level.
The level-one curve is the minimal/no-level coarse modular curve, and the map z↦e^{1/z} is used to show extension fails without the torsion-free hypothesis.
Y(1) is the minimal no-level modular curve A^1, and e^{1/z} is used to refute extension without torsion-freeness
the level-one modular curve Y(1)≅A1 is the minimal/no-level modular curve with torsion, and an essential-singularity map is used to show extension can fail
the minimal non-neat level-one modular curve Y(1)≅A1 with z↦e^{1/z} is used to show extension can fail without the torsion-free hypothesis
The level-one modular curve is the minimal no-level case, and the essential singularity map is used to show extension fails without the torsion-free hypothesis.
The level-one modular curve Y(1)≅A^1 with z↦e^{1/z} is used to show extension fails without the torsion-free hypothesis, and level one is the minimal non-neat/no-level case.
The level-one curve Y(1)≅A¹ with z↦e^{1/z} is used to show Borel extension fails without torsion-freeness, and level one is the minimal/no-level case.
the minimal/no-level j-line with the map z↦exp(1/z) is used to show extension can fail without the torsion-free hypothesis
Y(1) is the minimal/no-level modular curve, and the map z↦exp(1/z) is used to show extension fails without the torsion-free hypothesis.
The level-one/no-level modular curve is the minimal coarse case Y(1)≅A¹, and exp(1/z) is used to show extension fails without torsion-free level.
The level-one curve Y(1)≅A¹ is the no-level/minimal modular curve, and an essential-singularity map such as z↦e^{1/z} fails to extend, showing the torsion-free hypothesis is needed.

examples.name: Level-two modular curve as Big Picard
examples.note: The level-two modular curve P^1 minus three points is used to identify Borel extension in this case with the classical Big Picard theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker identifies the level-two modular curve with P1 minus three points and states its relation to Big Picard, but does not prove or work through the result on this example

passes.reason: Y(2)≅P¹ minus three points is the threshold hyperbolic punctured sphere appearing as the Big Picard case, not a failure example.
Y(2)≅P^1 minus three points is the critical minimal hyperbolic punctured-sphere case underlying Big Picard, used as a positive instance of the extension theorem.
Y(2)=P^1 minus three points is the critical three-puncture Picard threshold, used as a positive reformulation of Big Picard
Y(2)≅P1 minus three points is the minimal hyperbolic punctured sphere/Big-Picard threshold, cited as a positive extension case
Y(2)≅P1 minus three points is the critical three-omitted-values case underlying Big Picard, illustrating extension rather than refuting it
The level-two curve is P1 minus three points, the sharp Picard-threshold hyperbolic punctured sphere, used as an illustration of the extension theorem.
The level-two curve Y(2)≅P^1\{0,1,∞} is used as the positive Big Picard case and is the critical minimal punctured sphere where the Picard extension phenomenon begins.
Y(2)≅P¹ minus three points is presented as the Big Picard threshold case, the minimal hyperbolic punctured sphere rather than a counterexample.
Y(2)=P1 minus three points is used as the critical Big Picard threshold case for extension, not as a failure example
Y(2)≅P^1 minus three points is the minimal hyperbolic punctured sphere, used as the positive Big Picard threshold case.
Y(2)≅P¹ minus three points is the critical minimal hyperbolic Picard-threshold curve, used as a positive instance of the extension theorem.
The level-two curve Y(2)≅P¹\{0,1,∞} is the minimal/critical hyperbolic punctured sphere appearing in the Big Picard threshold, used as a positive instance of the extension theorem.

examples.name: Analytification of the affine line
examples.note: In response to a question, the affine line is described as the increasing union of closed disks, clarifying what the analytic space V^an means in the p-adic algebraization statement.
